title: Сравнение строк: ordinal vs culture и StringComparison

cards.prompt: Ordinal comparison is by code point: Compare("abc","ABC",Ordinal) is 'a'-'A' = 32, OrdinalIgnoreCase collapses ASCII case so Compare is 0 and Equals is True, printing 32 0 True
cards.code: using System; Console.WriteLine(string.Compare("abc", "ABC", StringComparison.Ordinal)); Console.WriteLine(string.Compare("abc", "ABC", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase)); Console.WriteLine(string.Equals("abc", "ABC", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase));

cards.prompt: The Turkish-I: one "i"/"I" pair, three policies, three verdicts - OrdinalIgnoreCase treats ASCII i/I as equal (True), the tr-TR culture treats them as different letters even ignoring case (Compare 1), and the invariant culture treats them as equal (Compare 0), printing True 1 0
cards.code: using System; using System.Globalization; var tr = new CultureInfo("tr-TR"); Console.WriteLine(string.Equals("i", "I", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase)); Console.WriteLine(string.Compare("i", "I", tr, CompareOptions.IgnoreCase)); Console.WriteLine(string.Compare("i", "I", CultureInfo.InvariantCulture, CompareOptions.IgnoreCase));

cards.prompt: Ordinal and culture sort disagree on sign: Compare("apple","Apple",Ordinal) is 32 because lowercase 'a' (97) has a higher code point than 'A' (65), but the en-US linguistic word sort places lowercase before uppercase giving -1, printing 32 then -1
cards.code: using System; using System.Globalization; var en = new CultureInfo("en-US"); Console.WriteLine(string.Compare("apple", "Apple", StringComparison.Ordinal)); Console.WriteLine(string.Compare("apple", "Apple", en, CompareOptions.None));
